data.title: BlackRock Owns The World
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

A 2019 study by Harvard Business Review found either Vanguard, BlackRock or State Street is the largest listed owner of 88% of S&P 500 companies.

Source: U.S. News

There is a perception that a few select companies own a vast majority of the stock market. While this perception is easily understood, especially when we see information like this from U.S. News or due to a Harvard Business Review study, the issue with it is that they're assuming these companies exert massive levels of control over the companies they have ownership of.

You have to remember that all three of these companies own this much of the stock market because of you, me, and millions of other Americans who are investing in passive market-wide ETFs through their 401Ks or their portfolios. Warren Buffett famously encouraged investors to predominantly invest in passive market-wide ETFs. Today, we have a concentration of managed capital among a few select names. It's not uncommon if you read articles on Seeking Alpha or other websites that ownership of a company will be discouraged because it can't beat the S&P 500 (SP500) during a certain time frame. The investor will simply say you should have just bought the S&P 500. Yet there are many people who, at the same time, say they dislike how much influence these three big companies have. They are encouraging others to provide those companies with more influence, all the same. It's a dichotomy that I find extremely interesting.

Another famous phrase in history says, "If you can't beat 'em, join 'em."

Today, I want to look at one of these three major companies.

Let's dive in!

The 800-Pound Gorilla Of The Asset Management Industry

BlackRock (BLK) is a name with which I can safely assume everyone reading is familiar. Paraphrasing Kody's most recent article, BlackRock is the undisputed leader of the global asset management industry.

Through its iShares brand of exchange-traded funds, it's the largest issuer of ETFs in the world. The company is also known for its Aladdin platform, which is a proprietary investment management software program used by large financial institutions around the world. All told, BlackRock held just shy of $13.5 trillion in assets under management as of Sept. 30, 2025.

Overall, it stands to cash in on both macro tailwinds and company-specific growth catalysts. In a September article on Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), Kody noted how total assets under management are projected to double to around $300 trillion by 2032. By itself, this macro environment, driven by expected economic growth, would be a major secular boost for BlackRock.

As the king of the global asset management industry, there is arguably nobody else who's going to cash in on this trend as much as BlackRock. According to the Investor Day 2025 Presentation, approximately 78% of its five-year active AUM came in above its peer median/benchmark. For a company with its sheer volume of AUM, this is an impressive feat.

That's why I believe that BlackRock can not only maintain its share of global AUM but grow it over time. This is how the company thinks that it can compound its revenue by about 10% annually from $20 billion in 2024 to $35 billion or more by 2030. Adjusted operating margins are expected to hold around 45%.

As a result, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for BlackRock is for 12.2% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth through 2027, off a 2024 base of $43.61.

The company is a financial tank to boot. It sports an AA- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

BlackRock's 1.9% dividend yield also registers above the S&P 500 index's yield. The 5-year CAGR of 7.5% is more modest. However, with the adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio likely to be in the low-40% range for 2025, BlackRock should soon return to more vigorous payout growth. This will allow it to lengthen its 16-year dividend growth streak.

BlackRock stock appears to currently be trading right around fair value. That jives with my goal to buy wonderful businesses at or below fair value. From the $1,091 share price, BlackRock's shares are priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 20.2.

That's precisely in line with the 10-year average P/E ratio. Given the company's encouraging, forward-looking fundamentals, I believe such a valuation can be supported in the years ahead.

If BlackRock can meet growth expectations and remain at fair value, it could post a 5% total return through 2026. By the end of 2030, annual total returns would be more compelling at around 11%.

As Kody recently put it, noteworthy risks to BlackRock include a prolonged market downturn and an incident that damages client trust.

As an asset manager, the company's fortunes hinge on the broader economy and key performance indicators, including assets under management. Thus, an economic recession or bear market could hurt its financial results.

Another risk to BlackRock is a potential loss of client trust/preference. In the asset management industry, client trust and preference are the most important currencies. A significant cyber breach or a failure of its funds to outperform their benchmarks could prompt clients to head for the exits. If BlackRock can't offset that with new clients, its fundamentals could be damaged.

Conclusion

BlackRock is an exceptionally well-run asset manager. Because it is exceptionally well run, it enjoys a massive degree of prominence and influence within the world economy. For many, they find this to be unattractive and want there to be a greater diversification among those who have the primacy of influence. On the other hand, many struggle to find opportunities that BlackRock or its competitors can find because they have the capital to leverage deals others could never touch.

When we look at the fundamental performance and health of BlackRock, we see that there is a great opportunity for future returns as they continue to expand and grow. For many, the question becomes, do you avoid BlackRock because of its prominence as a viewpoint against them, or do you invest in BlackRock because of its prominence when it's trading hands at an attractive value?

In the end, we at Dividend Kings believe that you should rule your portfolio. You should have clarity, not confusion. This means not allowing others' goals, viewpoints, and opinions to cloud your ability to make a sound judgment call based on fundamental health and research. Dividend investors perform well over the long run because they take a long-term investing viewpoint instead of a short-term trading viewpoint. A dividend portfolio operates best when its holdings are held for a long time, when dividends are growing, and total returns are achieved spectacularly.

Invest like royalty.

data.title: GDXJ: Gold Miners Hit The Jackpot; Ride The Trend, Beware The End
data.description:
In investing they often say, the trend is your friend. In other words, one can often make more from an investment by letting your winners ride rather than selling too soon and missing out on more gains down the road. On the other hand, many great investors have made a lot of money by “selling too soon.”

The main purpose of the stock market is to make fools of as many men as possible.

Bernard Baruch

In the case of the gold market, the trend in 2025 has definitely been rising gold prices. Gold set a record high above $4,400 an ounce in late December and is showing bullish momentum heading into 2026. In fact, gold equities, including the stocks of gold (and silver) miners, outperformed nearly all other asset classes in 2025. One way to invest in this asset class is by acquiring shares of an ETF that invests in “junior” gold miners, VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ).

Back in August of this year I wrote an educational piece on GDXJ, which you can read to get more of the details regarding why it may make sense to include the ETF in your growth-oriented investment portfolio. If you agree that the trend in higher gold prices is likely to continue into the new year, then it may still be a good time to buy GDXJ. I am not completely convinced that gold prices will remain elevated indefinitely, though, and caution is warranted when a correction in the price of gold is on the horizon, especially when investing in the junior miners. They will be the first to get impacted by any sudden shift in momentum, and I therefore rate GDXJ a Hold.

Bull Case for Higher Gold Prices in 2026

Interest rate cuts and increasing geopolitical tensions are just some of the factors driving up demand for gold and silver. China has also been a big buyer of gold in 2025, with its gold reserves rising every month in 2025 (through October), as shown in the chart below from the World Gold Council.

According to this chart from Visual Capitalist, Russia has also been stockpiling gold reserves in 2025.

Since the turn of the century, central banks have been steadily increasing their gold reserves, a trend that has sharply accelerated in the last few years. As global trust in traditional reserve currencies like the U.S. dollar is being tested by inflation, sanctions, and shifting alliances, many nations are turning to gold as a strategic store of value.

The primary reason why Russia and China are stocking up on gold is largely due to the devalued US dollar and a desire to be less reliant on the USD, as well as a desire by China to internationalize the yuan as a global currency alternative. As the price of the USD has declined, the price of gold has shot higher in the past year, as illustrated in this 5-year chart showing the value of the dollar (DXY) compared to gold prices as represented by SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD).

Emerging economies, including India, Turkey, Poland, and other central banks in emerging nations, are also adding to their gold reserves, putting additional upward price pressure on gold. This trend is expected to continue into 2026.

Analysts at top banks like UBS and Deutsche Bank are bullish on gold heading into 2026 as well.

Deutsche Bank lifted its 2026 forecast to $4,450 an ounce, up from $4,000 and is flagged the possibility of its spot price (XAUUSD:CUR) jumping to $5,000 due to stabilizing investor flow, and Q3 supply-demand data, including demand from central banks and ETF investments, according to Deutsche Bank analyst Michael Hsueh.

Similarly, UBS analysts raised their price target to $4,900/oz, up from $4,700/oz, rating gold as attractive and maintaining their long positions thanks to Federal Reserve rate cuts that lowers real yields, persistent geopolitical issues, the U.S. mid-term elections, and other fiscal concerns.

Seeking Alpha compiled a list of 30 top gold stocks based on YTD 2025 performance to take advantage of this bullish sentiment. Comparing that list to the top holdings in GDXJ (shown below), we find IAMGOLD Corporation (IAG) up 211% YTD, Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE) up 200%, Equinox Gold Corp. (EQX) up 176%, Pan American Silver Corp. (PAAS) up 147%, Alamos Gold Inc. (AGI) up 104%, and Royal Gold, Inc. (RGLD) up 68%.

Other top holdings that are not gold miners per se but mine silver or other precious metals are also showing tremendous YTD returns, including Hecla Mining (HL), up 301%; Endeavour Silver, up 163%; and Evolution Mining Limited, up 185%.

As a result, the ETF delivered a YTD total return of about 178% as of 12/24/25.

China’s Grip on the Gold Market

Expanding on this notion that China is influencing gold prices by substantially increasing its central bank reserves, I came across this in-depth discussion that shines some light on this recent trend that suggests a whole new monetary era is being ushered in.

Without a doubt, the Chinese central bank (PBOC) is still the leading single entity that is driving up the gold price to record highs, year-to-date by more than 55 percent. In the third quarter of 2025, the PBoC’s gold purchases (reported and unreported) accounted for 118 tonnes, up 39% MoM and 55% YoY, according to my long-time methodology.

Of course, some of the “unreported buying” mentioned by this author could also be coming from Russia. Regardless, let the record show that gold purchases by central banks have been increasing dramatically in the past several years, with a sharp rise in the past two years.

Rising Silver Prices, Too

In a comment on that article, it was mentioned that silver prices have also been rising substantially in 2025, and as noted above, many of the top holdings in GDXJ include silver miners. That comment suggests that Russia, by including silver in their official state reserve strategy; India, with record-breaking silver imports and silver demand in 2025; and Saudi Arabia, with their desire to diversify their sovereign wealth from oil into other diversified assets, are all major contributors to rising silver prices.

Silver just breached an all-time high of $70 an ounce after surging YTD by 140%. According to one analyst writing on December 10, silver remains bullish heading into 2026, but not without risks.

Silver has more than doubled in 2025, reaching a new all-time high near $60 on a "rare alignment of monetary, structural, and physical-market forces," and looking ahead to 2026, the bullish case remains intact but faces some risks, Saxo Bank said on Wednesday. Tight supply, price-inelastic industrial demand, and policy-driven dislocations have amplified silver's rally well beyond what gold (XAUUSD:CUR) alone would normally justify, Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen said. Monetary support also met a tightening physical market, as miners struggled to meet rising demand from electrification, solar, EVs, and data centers—an imbalance highlighted when silver was added to the US critical minerals list.

But there are risks:

A sharp slowdown in AI-related investment—potentially triggered by a correction in stretched valuations—could soften demand for chips and data center infrastructure while weighing on broader risk sentiment, Hansen noted.

Silver, as represented by the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV), is up over 140% in the past year as of 12/24/25.

The question heading into 2026 is will the momentum continue, and for how long? I cannot answer that question for you.

Lower Interest Rates, Too?

Another thing that benefits junior gold miners in particular is lower short-term interest rates. With the Fed lowering the rate below 4% and with more rate cuts potentially in store for 2026, GDXJ is likely to see some price appreciation for that reason, even if more cuts never happen. Investors will speculate that the costs for miners will go down while the prices of the commodities are going up, which is a formula for success. With rates already lower than they were just a few months ago, I expect that both Q4 2025 and Q1 2026 will be good ones for the miners when they report earnings.

However, I would suggest lowering your expectations after that, depending on what happens in the first couple of months of the new year. Prices tend to correct when they get overbought, and although I am not a technical analyst, this is not my first rodeo.

Conclusion: Momentum Suggests Buying GDXJ, But Prepare to Exit Swiftly

The prices of most investments in the stock market are driven largely by investor sentiment. Gold and silver prices are also significantly influenced by market sentiment, among many other factors, including the notion of a “safe haven” in a chaotic world. The more conflict that arises, the more demand there is for safe haven assets like gold and silver. Without taking a political stance, I think that one thing most can agree on is that there is rising global tension between the US, China, Russia, Europe, and other foreign nations and that trend is likely to continue into 2026.

For that reason, and with lower interest rates and the possibility of more rate cuts coming next year, I do believe that GDXJ has additional upside potential from further increases in gold and silver prices with lower costs of operations for the miners, at least in the first half of 2026. For novice investors who are looking to make a quick buck, however, I would suggest exercising extreme caution and perhaps using stop-loss orders if you are buying shares of GDXJ at what is already an elevated price. When the momentum shifts, it can shift quickly!

data.title: Forget Big Tech - I Think We're Looking At A Big Rotation To Value
data.description:
Introduction

Before we dive in, I hope everyone had a great Christmas Day yesterday. And for the people living in countries with two Christmas Days, I hope you have a great day today as well! Merry Christmas!

Although this is a time for many to take a step back, I decided to update readers on one of my most important theses, the one that combines the rotation from growth to value, the AI data center buildout, and my "bet" on cyclical value. As I have written in prior articles, it all seems to be intertwined, as many forces influence each other, creating a complex net that is somewhat tricky to understand for most investors.

While I will not claim that I have found the key to unlimited riches, I am pretty happy with the way things have played out in recent weeks, which is why I am using this article to update readers on my view that we are likely in the early stages of a wealth rotation that could provide us with plenty of opportunities to generate substantial alpha. This applies to both growth- and income-focused investors.

Today, we'll discuss the massively important role of artificial intelligence in the macro picture, leading economic growth indicators, and investment ideas.

So, as we have a lot to work through, let's get right to it!

It's All About Big Ideas at the Long End of the Equity Yield Curve

I have spent a lot of time this year discussing the rotation. While I'm a bit too lazy (because of the holidays, of course) to find out the exact statistics, I think I have written at least one big article per week in recent months.

That's not because I'm running out of ideas but because these subjects are absolutely crucial. After all, we're not talking about the future of a few random stocks or a thesis in a very specific niche. This is about the future of the economy and how asset classes trade, especially in relation to each other.

It's all about the Big Picture. I am convinced that if we get the market's biggest developments even roughly right (we don't have to be PhD-level geniuses or well-connected investors to understand these trends), we can often end up with highly favorable results.

One of the best examples of a Big Picture development is the rising gap between value and growth stocks since 2015. As we can see in the handy chart below, S&P 500 growth stocks have returned 380% since the end of 2015. That's almost 5x. Value stocks have returned 205%.

I'm looking for these investments following the Equity Yield Curve method. While I wish I had come up with that name, it's a concept developed by Horizon Kinetics, which I discussed in a recent strategy article. This model basically says that because the market is extremely focused on short-term developments, investors can find opportunities when adopting a long-term mindset. That's based on a number of factors, including the fact that most portfolio managers and analysts are driven by short-term performance measures and have to please investors on a very consistent basis.

For example, if you had run a fund without tech exposure in this market, odds are you would have gotten a lot of angry calls from investors asking you why the fund holds no shares in some of the most successful hyperscalers and semiconductor stocks.

This year, the market was all over AI, which makes sense, as the economy, as a whole, wasn't doing well. Just think about it:

  • The housing market is extremely weak due to high mortgage rates and elevated home prices that have created big affordability issues.
  • A large part of the American consumer is suffering due to prolonged sticky inflation and pressure on the labor market.
  • Manufacturing that's not directly related to AI did horribly.

In other words, it was an easy choice for the market to invest in Big Tech, as these companies have healthy balance sheets, AI tailwinds, and valuations that make sense as long as the market believes it's the only game in town.

Now, that's changing.

The Slow and Messy Rotation That Matters So Much to Investors and the Market

One of the indicators I have used a lot in the past is the ISM Manufacturing Index. This is a so-called Purchasing Managers' Index ("PMI"). It's a forward-looking survey based on, you guessed it, purchasing managers. As someone who holds a master's in Purchasing & Supply Management, I agree with everyone who says that these people have the best intel, as they are responsible for buying new supplies.

Whether it's a giant like Apple (AAPL) or a company like Caterpillar (CAT), purchasing managers will be the first to be upbeat when they sense that demand for new supplies is improving.

A funny story is that when COVID hit, I was a master's student. While the market didn't care when the first headlines came out about a new virus, my professor, who enjoys a terrific reputation in the industry, sold his shares, as his network told him that companies had become careful.

He wasn't a trader, yet that decision greatly benefited him.

Anyway, a PMI above 50 indicates economic growth. Since peaking in 2021, the ISM Manufacturing Index has been a hot mess, as we can see below.

This basically kept large investors (who all follow this number, I can tell you) at bay and out of most cyclical stocks. The best proof of that thesis is the correlation between the year-on-year performance of the Union Pacific (UNP) stock price and the ISM Manufacturing Index (black line) below.

It's almost perfect, as Union Pacific is a cyclical railroad.

On top of the ISM Index, I use a wide range of other leading indicators, including the average of the Philadelphia and New York Federal Reserve manufacturing surveys. Although that number is a lot more volatile than the ISM number, as we can see below, it tends to capture cycles extremely well.

Last month, it did well, as it surged by a few points. Back then, I used it as a reason to validate my thesis that cyclical growth is bottoming.

Now (December), it's showing a steep decline:

A number of readers asked me about this in comments and direct messages.

Although I obviously would have preferred a further upswing (it would have made me look really smart - I'm joking), this isn't a reversal, as new orders were strong, hiring plans picked up, and future outlook components remained very solid.

Basically, the call is volatile, which is always the case at turning points. What matters is that long-term averages remain in a bottoming phase.

For now, I expect 2026 to see a full-blown upswing in PMIs.

To give you some more proof of my thesis (the point of why I'm writing this article), I want to include some comments from Jordi Visser, whom I have brought up in prior articles as well. Although I didn't get my cyclical value thesis from him, I give him tremendous credit for bringing the impact of AI on that thesis to my attention.

There are two critical things to keep in mind here. The first thing is AI-related CapEx, which is basically QE-like liquidity for the industrial supply chain.

The five biggest hyperscalers alone are expected to boost annual CapEx from $211 billion in 2024 to $540 billion by 2027. In other words, 2025 was the first year that we actually witnessed a massive wave of capital hit the economy in the form of accelerated data center construction. This is just the start.

The second point is that AI spending has reached bottlenecks. This is something I have often brought up related to actual data center construction, as hyperscalers have run into huge delays for critical equipment like natural gas turbines and actual power supplies. On top of that, the grid is often old and requires massive amounts of spending (estimates vary a lot here).

Below, you're looking at a screenshot/quote shared by Bloomberg's Joe Weisenthal, which perfectly shows just how big these issues have become:

Especially once AI becomes tangible and robotics become mainstream, I think the demand for power and actual materials will skyrocket. As these companies have a much bigger weighting in manufacturing PMIs than asset-light software companies, it will almost certainly have a big impact.

Right now, I am convinced that we are in a situation where the market is figuring this out. One reason why I believe that is because it is much more critical of AI stocks. For example, players like Oracle (ORCL), which are in a period of rapid spending, will have to prove themselves in the years ahead.

In other words, investors are not willing to throw money at everything with an AI tailwind. They want to know how this is going to lead to actual financial returns.

Before I elaborate on this, the chart below shows the ISM Manufacturing Index. That's the orange line. It's being compared to the white line that displays year-over-year growth in Taiwanese exports.

As you may know, Taiwan's exports are all about electronics. Here's a list of its main export goods, according to its Wikipedia page:

Semiconductors, petrochemicals, automobile/auto parts, ships, wireless communication equipment, flat panel displays, steel, electronics, plastics, computers.

Using Visser's words (I'm paraphrasing), this is clear evidence that the AI revolution has reached its physical constraints, which are needed to accelerate its innovative power. Now, it's likely that PMIs accelerate.

Other factors to keep in mind are deregulation and lower interest rates, which fit my "running it hot" thesis. Basically, it implies the government and the Fed will accept higher inflation if it allows them to implement measures that achieve broader and stronger nominal economic growth. Think of a stronger housing market. The other day, I included the quote below in one of my articles on this topic:

Lately, all these factors are keeping rates high. Inflation appears to be holding steady at 3%, yet the Fed has already started easing by cutting rates and ending quantitative tightening. There is anxiety about inflation staying high because the full impact of tariffs has yet to be felt, and the Fed has not indicated (short of some wish-casting in its summary of economic projections) that it is committed to bringing inflation down further. There is even less commitment in Congress to reducing the debt, which means more bonds will be issued in the future, which means a drop in bond prices. This makes holding longer-term bonds riskier, and investors want a bigger premium to compensate for that. - Bloomberg

If I'm right, there will likely (read: almost certainly) be consequences for the market.

At the start of this article, I brought up the performance of growth and value stocks over the past ten years. In recent years, that gap has widened.

This includes the ratio between Russell 2000 (IWM) small-cap stocks and the S&P 500, which is displayed in the chart below.

This is the ratio between industrial stocks (XLI) and the S&P 500:

Once economic growth broadens, we will likely see a multi-trillion-dollar rotation, as tens of trillions have flown into Big Tech in recent years due to the safety aspect on top of AI being somewhat of the only game in town.

If a recession were to happen, this thesis would be void.

However, if I am even somewhat right and we get a cyclical rotation, I am convinced that many asset managers will be forced to rotate, as they are "all" (you know what I mean) massively overweight Big Tech stocks.

Right now, Bank of America data shows that fund manager positioning indicates expectations of a substantial economic growth recovery, as we can see below. All else excluded, the chart implies an ISM surge to roughly 60.

With that said, this chart is super important. One could even say it's one of the most important parts of my article. Basically, the chart above tells us that investors are extremely long stocks. This likely indicates that cash levels are subdued.

We also know that the market has massively favored Big Tech stocks.

Are you still with me?

What this means is that if the economy sees broadening strength, a rotation is even more likely, as money will have to come out of areas that have been favored in recent quarters (or even years).

Based on this alone, I think we can assume two things:

  • General market returns could be subdued, as so much has already been priced in. The chart below supports that thesis. Note that I am not calling for 0% long-term returns. All I am saying is that general market returns could be subdued.
  • If investors are forced to shift capital into other themes, a lot of money will likely benefit sectors and industries that have been left behind.

In other words, while this thesis is still in its very early stages (assuming I am right), new developments seem to support a mix of "running it hot" and broadening economic growth, all fueled by QE-like AI spending.

That's why I maintain a very balanced investment mix. While I like some Mag-7 stocks like Alphabet (GOOGL) and Amazon (AMZN), as I discussed in this article, I have been buying a lot more cyclical value lately. As I wrote in a recent portfolio review, I own a lot of transportation, housing, and energy. These are some of the areas where I expect to see a lot of alpha in the years ahead.

The other day, I made a screener that focused on undervalued cyclical stocks with a specific focus on very high returns on invested capital, meaning I filtered for some of the best-managed companies on the market.

These are the kinds of investments that I expect will allow us to beat the market going forward. One could even say they can help us beat the market when it matters most, as I think we discussed a lot of potential evidence that suggests the market won't be as much fun going forward as it was in recent years.

That brings me to the takeaway:

Takeaway

If there's just one thing you are going to remember from this article, let it be that we're likely looking at a major market shift. In recent weeks, we have gotten a lot more evidence that suggests investors aren't that eager anymore to throw loads of money into the AI part of the market.

Now, money seems to be trickling down, finding its way into areas with much better risk/rewards. If this move is supported by broadening economic growth, it could give investors a true incentive to start rotating substantial capital.

Personally, I believe a strong sign will be a potential ISM move above 50-52 in the next few months. That would basically provide validation that economic strength is broadening and allow investors to shift money, backed by fundamental "proof," so to speak.

Additionally, in this article, I put more focus on the impact of AI spending on growth. There is evidence to suggest AI innovation has now hit physical growth barriers (bottlenecks). Solving these will require a lot of capital that will end up flowing through the "real economy."

We're still in the early innings, but if I'm even remotely right about this, I think massive opportunities present themselves for investors to diversify in a market that has a high risk of showing subdued returns in the year(s) ahead.

data.title: Best Buy: A Beaten-Down, Attractively Priced, 5% Yielder Hiding In Plain Sight (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Although I haven't stepped foot in a Best Buy (BBY) for years, I've kept them on my radar. Despite lingering headwinds, the company has remained resilient over the past few years.

With interest rates remaining above historical levels and tariffs impacting the business, I've been impressed with the improvements the retailer has made in the past year.

Additionally, the stock appears attractively priced and sports a dividend yield above 5%. During my last thesis, I had concerns about the company's dividend safety.

Since then, they've navigated headwinds, ensuring the dividend is likely to remain safe, at least for the near to medium term. Moreover, with AI enthusiasm at all-time highs, BBY appears to be an attractively priced income play hiding in plain sight.

In this article, I discuss Best Buy's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm upgrading the stock from a hold to a buy.

Previous Hold Thesis

It's been over a year since I last covered Best Buy in an article titled: Impressive Bull Run But Dividend Safety Remains A Concern.

At the time, BBY experienced a 40% price rally, likely due to their resilient business, positive outlook, and raised guidance. However, due to the dividend safety concerns and valuation above their historical average, I thought the rally was unsupported.

Since then, the stock has significantly underperformed the S&P in the past 14 months (SP500), down more than 28% at the time of writing. In comparison, the index is up close to 18% over the same period.

Q3 Earnings Show Improvements

Best Buy announced their FY'26 Q3 earnings at the end of November and managed to beat analysts' estimates on both their top and bottom lines. EPS of $1.40 beat by a sizable $0.09, while revenue was $9.7 billion, beating by $80 million.

This was the second consecutive double-beat for the fiscal year, showing the company's resilient business amidst uncertainty. In Q1, they managed to beat their bottom lines but missed top-line estimates by $50 million.

So, a solid performance all around for the retailer, if you ask me. EPS grew quarter-over-quarter and from the prior year by double digits. This amounted to $1.28 in Q2 and $1.26 in the previous year's quarter.

Sales were up from $9.4 billion the prior quarter and 2.3% year-over-year. BBY's performance was driven by increasing revenue within their segments and better-than-expected comparable store sales.

Comparable store sales increased 2.7%, opposite from the prior year's quarter that saw these decline 2.9%, driven by softer consumer demand. And with interest rates down significantly from last year and expected to decline by the end of 2026, BBY is likely to see tailwinds.

And over the next year or two I suspect they can see more improvements as consumers feel relief from lower base rates. Below is how the company fared in each of their segments. Although some still experienced declines, in comparison to last year, the performance was significantly better.

Domestic, Consumer Electronics, Appliances, and Entertainment all saw declines, but lesser in comparison to the prior year's quarter. But revenue for the segment still grew 2.1% to $8.9 billion. However, Domestic margins were still pressured, down 30 basis points year-over-year to 23.3%.

In their International segment, revenue growth was nearly 3x higher at 6.1%. This stood at $794 million, driven by solid growth in comparable store sales of 6.3% and Best Buy Express locations. Gross margins increased 30 basis points to 22.8%.

So, while headwinds still weigh on the retailer, BBY has successfully navigated while managing to grow earnings and make improvements to the business.

Raised Guidance

After their successful third quarter, Best Buy raised guidance for the fiscal year. This was after lowering guidance subsequent to Q1 earnings. Sales guidance was raised to $41.65 billion to $41.95 billion, up from $41.1 billion to $41.9 billion. This represents a slight increase from the $41.5 billion they saw last fiscal year.

Comparable sales guidance was also raised, now expected in a range of 0.5% to 1.2%, up from negative 1% to 1%. EPS is now expected to be between $6.25 and $6.35, up from $6.15 and $6.30.

This is a significant increase from last year's $4.28, with frequent share repurchases being a likely driver.

While the comparable sales guidance is still a bit disappointing, it shows BBY is trending in the right direction, solid considering the tough economic backdrop.

Balance Sheet & Dividend

Something I also respected about BBY was the company's solid balance sheet. This does give them some flexibility when I questioned their dividend sustainability.

The company at the end of Q3 only had $1.15 billion in long-term debt, small in comparison to their market cap of nearly $15 billion.

Liquidity was robust with $1.194 billion in cash & equivalents. Furthermore, the company continues to repurchase shares, also a testament to their balance sheet health.

During the quarter, they bought back $35 million worth of shares, returning a total of $234 million to shareholders in the form of dividends and buybacks. Year-to-date, BBY has paid $602 million in dividends and returned $802 million to shareholders.

For the fiscal year, management stated that they still plan to repurchase $300 million worth of shares. And these will likely drive solid earnings growth for the foreseeable future. These also give the company flexibility by enhancing their dividend safety.

Since my last coverage, BBY raised the dividend by a penny to $0.95 per share. And because headwinds will likely take some time to dissipate, I expect similar increases until macro conditions improve exponentially, likely over the next 2 to 3 years.

Regarding their dividend safety, even if the company didn't repurchase any more shares, they would only need roughly $800 million in free cash flow to cover the dividend using their shares outstanding.

Last fiscal year, free cash flow was $1.4 billion, with $807 million in dividends paid, giving the company a safe payout ratio. This also gives the retailer ample retained cash flow to reinvest back into the business.

Appears Fairly Valued

Best Buy can experience further volatility, especially if the economy weakens going into 2026. But using their guidance midpoint of $6.30, this gives BBY a forward P/E of just 11.04x, attractive for a company of their caliber.

This is significantly below the sector median's 17.87x and slightly below their 5-year average. For comparison, BBY also trades below peer DICK'S Sporting Goods' (DKS) forward multiple of 14.44x.

At the current price, BBY also offers more than 35% upside with a price target of $94.31 over the next two years if they return to their normal multiple. For the near term, I expect any significant upside will be limited until they see sequential improvements in sales and further improvements within their segments.

Risks & Bottom Line

Although U.S. consumers have continued to show resilience, further upticks in job losses going into 2026 could have a continued impact on their Domestic segments. Unemployment currently stands at 4.6%.

And with immigration crackdowns and massive company layoffs, this could rise further in the coming months. If so, these could result in further downside if additional segments see declines.

But with the price down close to 25% in the past 14 months, many of the company's headwinds are likely already priced in. Furthermore, BBY continues to recover despite challenges.

And with lower base rates likely having a positive impact on consumers, the retailer could be poised for strong upside over the next 1 to 2 years should macro conditions continue to improve. Due to their yield above 5% and attractive valuation below the peer average, I am upgrading Best Buy from a hold to a buy.
